chunk_summary: This section evaluates menstrual irregularities in adolescents, emphasizing nutrition-linked causes like RED-S and PCOS, and provides structured diagnostic and management strategies based on gynecologic age and clinical context.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of gynecologic age in evaluating menstrual irregularities in adolescents.
Identify nutrition-linked causes of menstrual irregularities, including RED-S and PCOS.
Apply structured diagnostic approaches for secondary amenorrhea and oligomenorrhea.
Develop nutrition and lifestyle interventions tailored to RED-S and PCOS management.
key_facts: Menstrual irregularity should be assessed as a clinical vital sign, not dismissed as normal puberty.
RED-S can occur even with normal BMI and requires increased energy availability, reduced training load, and calcium/vitamin D adequacy.
Adolescent PCOS diagnosis requires persistent menstrual irregularity and clinical/biochemical hyperandrogenism, excluding mimics; ultrasound is not diagnostic within 8 years post-menarche.
Obesity-related PCOS management includes sustainable dietary changes, daily physical activity, and metabolic comorbidity screening per IAP guidelines.
Structured differential diagnoses for menstrual irregularities include thyroid disease, hyperprolactinemia, hypothalamic suppression, and primary ovarian insufficiency.
reference_quote: Menstruation in girls and adolescents should be treated as a vital sign, with persistent irregularity warranting structured evaluation and management. (ACOG)
document_key_topics: Nutrition-linked causes of amenorrhea and oligomenorrhea
PCOS diagnosis and management in adolescents
